---
title: Bayesian mixed effects modeling of antimicrobial MIC data with censoring in
  brms
author: "Dylan Boone"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
toc_float: true
theme: flatly
code_folding: hide
---

## Introduction

Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) assays are a cornerstone of microbiology 
research. Dating back to the golden age of antibiotics, MIC assays remain essential 
for determining the lowest concentration of a compound that inhibits bacterial growth. 
Despite decades of methodological advancement in microbiology, the statistical 
treatment of MIC data has largely lagged behind. MIC values are conventionally 
treated as discrete measurements, when in reality the true MIC lies somewhere 
between two successive two-fold dilutions.

A further complication arises when strains exceed the highest concentration tested — 
a common occurrence in resistant organisms. These **right-censored** observations are 
typically discarded or treated as exact values, both of which introduce bias. 
Frequentist mixed effects frameworks such as `lme4` rely on maximum likelihood 
estimation and require exact observations, making them poorly suited for censored 
MIC data.

Here I develop a Bayesian hierarchical mixed effects model to estimate posterior 
median MIC values for 29 clinical and reference strains of *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* 
tested against two standard of care antibiotics — ceftazidime (CAZ) and ertapenem 
(ETP). A Bayesian framework naturally accommodates right-censored observations, 
propagating uncertainty from censored strains through to the final estimates rather 
than discarding or mishandling them. MIC data were obtained using the standard Broth 
Microdilution (BMD) assay in cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton broth (CAMHB).

## Statistical Methods

### Model Overview

All models were fit using the `brms` package (version 2.23.1) in R, with `cmdstanr` 
as the Stan backend. `brms` provides a flexible interface for fitting Bayesian 
generalized linear mixed effects models using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) sampling 
via Stan. Three models were fit iteratively, with each adding complexity to the 
previous to better capture the structure of the data.

---

### Outcome Transformation

Raw MIC values (µg/mL) were log2 transformed prior to modeling. MIC assays follow a 
two-fold dilution series, meaning successive concentrations double at each step. Log2 
transformation linearizes this scale such that each unit increase corresponds to one 
dilution step, satisfying the normality assumption of the Gaussian likelihood and 
making model coefficients directly interpretable in units of dilutions.

---

### Censoring

Of the 648 observations, 226 were right-censored at 256 µg/mL — the highest 
concentration tested. Rather than discarding these observations or treating them as 
exact values of 256 µg/mL (both of which introduce bias), censored observations were 
explicitly declared using the `cens()` response modifier in `brms`. This instructs the 
model to evaluate the likelihood as P(MIC > 256) for censored observations rather than 
P(MIC = 256), properly propagating uncertainty from censored strains through to the 
posterior estimates.

---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(brms)
library(tidybayes)
library(ggplot2)
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)

# Tell cmdstanr where to find cmdstan
library(cmdstanr)
set_cmdstan_path("~/.cmdstan/cmdstan-2.38.0")
```

### Model Structure

All three models shared the following general structure:

$$\text{log}_2(\text{MIC})_{ijk} = \mu + \beta_{\text{antibiotic}} + u_{\text{strain}_i} + u_{\text{run}_j} + u_{\text{operator}_k} + \epsilon_{ijk}$$

Where:
- $\mu$ is the grand mean log2 MIC for CAZ (reference level)
- $\beta_{\text{antibiotic}}$ is the fixed effect of antibiotic (ETP vs CAZ)
- $u_{\text{strain}_i} \sim N(0, \sigma^2_{\text{strain}})$ is the strain random intercept
- $u_{\text{run}_j} \sim N(0, \sigma^2_{\text{run}})$ is the run/day random intercept
- $u_{\text{operator}_k} \sim N(0, \sigma^2_{\text{operator}})$ is the operator random intercept
- $\epsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ is the residual error

CAZ was set as the reference level for the antibiotic factor, such that the intercept 
represents the grand mean CAZ MIC and $\beta_{\text{antibiotic}}$ represents the 
average ETP vs CAZ difference on the log2 scale.

---

```{Data preprocessing}
mic <- mic %>% mutate(
  log2mic = log2(mic_ug_ml),
  censored = ifelse(mic_censored == "right", "right", "none"),
  antibiotic = factor(antibiotic, levels = c("CAZ", "ETP")),
  operator = factor(operator),
  run_id = factor(run_id),
  strain = factor(strain)
)
```

### Model Iterations

**fit1** included random intercepts for strain, run, and operator, and a fixed effect 
for antibiotic. This model assumed the ETP vs CAZ difference was uniform across all 
strains.

**fit2** extended fit1 by adding a random slope for antibiotic within strain — 
`(antibiotic | strain)` — allowing each strain to have its own ETP vs CAZ difference. 
This captures strain-specific antibiotic resistance patterns that a fixed effect alone 
cannot. A prior on the correlation between strain intercepts and slopes was added using 
the LKJ distribution. `adapt_delta` was increased to 0.95 to address a small number 
of divergent transitions.

**fit3** extended fit2 by allowing the residual standard deviation (sigma) to vary by 
antibiotic — `sigma ~ antibiotic` — addressing the bimodal distribution observed in 
posterior predictive checks of fit1 and fit2. CAZ and ETP have substantially different 
MIC ranges, and modeling sigma separately for each antibiotic better reflects this 
heterogeneity in residual spread.

---

### Prior Specification

Priors were specified to reflect domain knowledge about *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* MIC 
values for CAZ and ETP:

| Parameter | Prior | Justification |
|-----------|-------|---------------|
| Intercept | Normal(5.6, 0.8) | Grand mean CAZ MIC expected ~32-64 µg/mL; log2(48) ≈ 5.6 |
| β~ETP~ | Normal(1.5, 0.5) | ETP expected 1-2 dilutions higher than CAZ; 1.5 is midpoint |
| σ~strain~ | Exponential(1) | Weakly informative; positive-only prior on variance component |
| σ~run~ | Exponential(1) | Weakly informative; positive-only prior on variance component |
| σ~operator~ | Exponential(1) | Weakly informative; positive-only prior on variance component |
| σ~residual~ | Exponential(1) | Weakly informative; positive-only prior on residual SD |
| Correlation | LKJ(2) | Weakly skeptical of extreme correlations between intercepts and slopes |
| σ submodel | Normal(0, 1) | Prior on log scale sigma coefficient for antibiotic effect on residual SD |

---

```{Priors}
priors <- c(
  prior(normal(5.6, 0.8), class = Intercept),
  prior(normal(1.5, 0.5), class = b, coef = antibioticETP),
  prior(exponential(1), class = sd, group = strain),
  prior(exponential(1), class = sd, group = run_id),
  prior(exponential(1), class = sd, group = operator),
  prior(exponential(1), class = sigma)
)
```

### MCMC Sampling and Convergence

All models were fit with 4 independent Markov chains, each running 4000 iterations 
with the first 1000 discarded as warmup, yielding 12000 total post-warmup draws. 
Chains were run in parallel across 4 CPU cores. A random seed of 42 was set for 
reproducibility.

Convergence was assessed using the potential scale reduction factor ($\hat{R}$) and 
effective sample size (ESS). All parameters achieved $\hat{R}$ = 1.00 and bulk ESS 
values well above 1000, indicating good convergence and efficient sampling. Trace 
plots were visually inspected and confirmed the characteristic "fuzzy caterpillar" 
appearance expected of well-mixed chains. A small number of divergent transitions 
were observed in fit2 (32/12000) and addressed by increasing `adapt_delta` to 0.95, 
reducing divergences to 3/12000 in the final run. fit3 divergences were negligible.


## Results

### Model 1 (fit1) — Random Intercepts

All four chains converged successfully with $\hat{R}$ = 1.00 for all parameters and 
bulk ESS values well above 1000, indicating efficient sampling and good convergence.

#### Variance Components

The dominant source of variation in log2 MIC was strain identity (SD = 2.78, 95% CrI: 
2.04–3.84), which is expected given the dataset includes both susceptible reference 
strains and MDR clinical isolates spanning several log2 dilutions in resistance. 

Day-to-day run variation was the second largest source of variability (SD = 0.81, 95% 
CrI: 0.30–1.83), corresponding to approximately 1 dilution of batch-to-batch 
variation. This likely reflects differences in media preparation, antibiotic stock 
age, and incubator conditions between experimental runs.

Operator variation was the smallest variance component (SD = 0.59, 95% CrI: 
0.25–1.39), contributing less than 1 dilution of variability across technicians. This 
is reassuring from a reproducibility standpoint, suggesting the BMD assay was 
performed consistently across operators.

Residual within-cell variation (sigma = 1.20, 95% CrI: 1.12–1.29) represents 
approximately 1 dilution of unexplained replicate-to-replicate noise remaining after 
accounting for all modeled sources of variation, consistent with typical BMD assay 
reproducibility.

#### Fixed Effects

The grand mean CAZ MIC (intercept) was estimated at 5.21 log2 µg/mL (95% CrI: 
4.00–6.39), corresponding to approximately 37 µg/mL on the original scale. ETP MICs 
were on average 2.16 log2 dilutions higher than CAZ (95% CrI: 1.14–3.04), equivalent 
to approximately 4.5-fold higher on the original concentration scale (2^2.16 ≈ 4.5 
µg/mL). The 95% credible interval for the ETP effect did not include zero, providing 
strong evidence of a meaningful difference in resistance between the two antibiotics 
across this strain collection.

```{Fit 1}
fit <- brm(
  log2mic | cens(censored) ~ antibiotic + (1 | strain) + (1 | run_id) + (1 | operator),
  data = mic,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = priors,
  chains = 4,
  iter = 4000,
  warmup = 1000,
  cores = 4,
  seed = 42,
  backend = "cmdstanr"
) 
```

```{Fit 1 Summary}
summary(fit) 
```

```{Fit 1 Diagnostic Plot}
plot(fit)
```

```{Fit 1 Posterior Median SD}
fit %>%
  spread_draws(r_strain[strain, term]) %>%
  filter(term == "Intercept") %>%
  median_qi(.width = c(0.95)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(y = reorder(strain, r_strain), x = r_strain, xmin = .lower, xmax = .upper)) +
  geom_pointrange() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(x = "Strain-level deviation from grand mean (log2 scale)",
       y = "Strain",
       title = "Strain random effects") +
  theme_bw()
```

```{Fit 1 Observed vs Simulated}
pp_check(fit, ndraws = 100)
```

### Model 2 (fit2) — Random Slopes for Antibiotic Within Strain

fit2 extended fit1 by adding a random slope for antibiotic within strain, allowing 
each strain to have its own ETP vs CAZ difference rather than assuming a uniform 
antibiotic effect across all strains. An LKJ(2) prior was added on the correlation 
between strain intercepts and slopes. `adapt_delta` was increased to 0.95 to address 
divergent transitions, reducing them from 32 to 3 out of 12000 total draws. All 
parameters achieved $\hat{R}$ = 1.00 with bulk ESS values well above 1000.

#### Variance Components

Strain intercept variation remained the dominant source of variability (SD = 2.84, 
95% CrI: 2.04–3.96), consistent with fit1. The addition of the random slope revealed 
substantial strain-to-strain variation in the ETP vs CAZ difference (SD = 1.92, 95% 
CrI: 1.23–2.88), indicating that the antibiotic effect is far from uniform across 
strains — some strains show dramatically higher ETP resistance relative to CAZ while 
others do not.

A moderate negative correlation was estimated between strain intercepts and ETP slopes 
(cor = -0.36, 95% CrI: -0.76–0.20), suggesting that strains with higher baseline CAZ 
MICs tend to show a slightly smaller strain-specific ETP effect. However, the credible 
interval crossed zero and this pattern should be interpreted cautiously.

Run variation decreased substantially compared to fit1 (SD = 0.33, 95% CrI: 
0.01–1.10), suggesting that some apparent day-to-day variation in fit1 was in fact 
strain-specific antibiotic variation now captured by the random slope. Operator 
variation remained small and consistent with fit1 (SD = 0.63, 95% CrI: 0.28–1.43).

Residual sigma decreased from 1.20 in fit1 to 1.07 (95% CrI: 0.99–1.14), indicating 
that the random slope explained additional variance beyond what the random intercept 
alone captured.

#### Fixed Effects

The grand mean CAZ intercept was stable relative to fit1 (5.29 log2 µg/mL, 95% CrI: 
4.10–6.41, ≈ 39 µg/mL). The average ETP effect was similarly consistent (2.18 log2 
dilutions, 95% CrI: 1.31–2.98), with a narrower credible interval than fit1 
reflecting improved model efficiency from the random slope structure. The 95% credible 
interval did not include zero, again providing strong evidence of a meaningful 
difference between antibiotics at the population level.

```{Fit 2 Priors}
priors2 <- c(
  prior(normal(5.6, 0.8), class = Intercept),
  prior(normal(1.5, 0.5), class = b, coef = antibioticETP),
  prior(exponential(1), class = sd, group = strain),
  prior(exponential(1), class = sd, group = run_id),
  prior(exponential(1), class = sd, group = operator),
  prior(exponential(1), class = sigma),
  prior(lkj(2), class = cor, group = strain)
)
```

```{Fit 2}
fit2 <- brm(
  log2mic | cens(censored) ~ antibiotic + (antibiotic | strain) + (1 | run_id) + (1 | operator),
  data = mic,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = priors2,
  chains = 4,
  iter = 4000,
  warmup = 1000,
  cores = 4,
  seed = 42,
  backend = "cmdstanr",
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.95)
)
```

```{Fit 2 Summary}
summary(fit2)
```

```{Fit 2 Diagnostic Plot}
plot(fit2)
```

```{Fit 2 Posterior Median SD}
fit2 %>%
  spread_draws(r_strain[strain, term]) %>%
  filter(term == "antibioticETP") %>%
  group_by(strain) %>%
  median_qi(r_strain, .width = 0.95) %>%
  arrange(desc(r_strain)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(y = reorder(strain, r_strain), x = r_strain, xmin = .lower, xmax = .upper)) +
  geom_pointrange() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(x = "Strain-specific ETP vs CAZ difference (log2 scale)",
       y = "Strain",
       title = "How much higher is ETP MIC vs CAZ MIC per strain?") +
  theme_bw()
```

### Model 3 (fit3) — Antibiotic-Specific Residual Variance

fit3 extended fit2 by allowing the residual standard deviation (sigma) to vary by 
antibiotic, addressing the bimodal distribution observed in posterior predictive checks 
of fit1 and fit2. This was implemented using a sigma submodel — `sigma ~ antibiotic` 
— via the `bf()` brmsformula wrapper. All parameters achieved $\hat{R}$ = 1.00 with 
bulk ESS values well above 1000, indicating good convergence.

#### Variance Components

Strain intercept variation remained the dominant source of variability and was 
consistent across all three models (SD = 2.90, 95% CrI: 2.08–4.02). Strain-to-strain 
variation in the ETP vs CAZ difference was similarly stable relative to fit2 
(SD = 1.85, 95% CrI: 1.16–2.77), confirming that strain-specific antibiotic 
resistance patterns are a genuine feature of the data rather than a modeling artifact.

The negative correlation between strain intercepts and ETP slopes strengthened 
slightly in fit3 (cor = -0.50, 95% CrI: -0.83–0.04), with the credible interval now 
nearly excluding zero. This provides stronger — though still cautious — evidence that 
strains with higher baseline CAZ resistance tend to show a smaller strain-specific ETP 
effect.

Run variation remained small and consistent with fit2 (SD = 0.33, 95% CrI: 
0.03–1.05). Operator variation was similarly small (SD = 0.68, 95% CrI: 0.31–1.52), 
continuing to suggest good reproducibility across technicians.

#### Fixed Effects

The grand mean CAZ intercept remained stable across all three models (5.29 log2 
µg/mL, 95% CrI: 4.12–6.40, ≈ 39 µg/mL). The average ETP fixed effect was consistent 
with fit1 and fit2 (2.11 log2 dilutions, 95% CrI: 1.26–2.90), again with the 95% 
credible interval excluding zero.

#### Sigma Submodel

The sigma submodel revealed meaningful differences in residual spread between the two 
antibiotics. The baseline sigma intercept on the log scale was 0.39 (95% CrI: 
0.28–0.51), corresponding to a CAZ residual SD of e^0.39 ≈ 1.48 log2 dilutions. The 
ETP sigma coefficient was -0.82 (95% CrI: -0.96 to -0.68), indicating that residual 
variation was substantially lower for ETP than CAZ — e^(0.39 - 0.82) ≈ 0.65 log2 
dilutions. This is consistent with the observation that ETP MICs were more 
concentrated at the upper end of the dilution range, particularly due to the high 
proportion of right-censored observations, while CAZ MICs were more spread across the 
full dilution range. The credible intervals for both sigma parameters excluded zero, 
providing strong evidence that residual variance differs meaningfully between 
antibiotics.

```{Fit 3 Priors}
priors3 <- c(
  prior(normal(5.6, 0.8), class = Intercept),
  prior(normal(1.5, 0.5), class = b, coef = antibioticETP),
  prior(exponential(1), class = sd, group = strain),
  prior(exponential(1), class = sd, group = run_id),
  prior(exponential(1), class = sd, group = operator),
  #prior(exponential(1), class = sigma),
  prior(lkj(2), class = cor, group = strain),
  prior(normal(0, 1), class = b, dpar = "sigma")
)
```

```{Fit 3}
fit3 <- brm(
  bf(log2mic | cens(censored) ~ antibiotic + (antibiotic | strain) + (1 | run_id) + (1 | operator),
     sigma ~ antibiotic),
  data = mic,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = priors3,
  chains = 4,
  iter = 4000,
  warmup = 1000,
  cores = 4,
  seed = 42,
  backend = "cmdstanr",
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.95)
)
```

```{Fit 3 summary}
summary(fit3)
```

```{Fit 3 Diagnostic Plot}
plot(fit3)
```

```{Fit 3 Posterior Median SD}
fit3 %>%
  spread_draws(r_strain[strain, term]) %>%
  filter(term == "Intercept") %>%
  median_qi(r_strain, .width = 0.95) %>%
  ggplot(aes(y = reorder(strain, r_strain), x = r_strain, xmin = .lower, xmax = .upper)) +
  geom_pointrange() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(x = "Strain-level deviation from grand mean (log2 scale)",
       y = "Strain",
       title = "Strain random effects - fit3") +
  theme_bw()
```

```{Fit 3 Posterior Medians for 29 strains}
fit3 %>%
  spread_draws(r_strain[strain, term], b_Intercept, b_antibioticETP) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = term, values_from = r_strain) %>%
  mutate(
    caz_log2 = b_Intercept + Intercept,
    etp_log2 = b_Intercept + b_antibioticETP + Intercept + antibioticETP,
    caz_mic = 2^caz_log2,
    etp_mic = 2^etp_log2
  ) %>%
  group_by(strain) %>%
  median_qi(caz_mic, etp_mic, .width = 0.95) %>%
  arrange(desc(caz_mic)) %>%
  print(n = 29)
```

```{Fit 3 Posterior Observed vs Simulated Plot}
pp_check(fit3, ndraws = 100)
```

```{Linearity Check}
res <- residuals(fit3)
res_df <- as.data.frame(res)

ggplot(res_df, aes(x = Estimate)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(x = "Residuals", title = "Residual distribution - fit3") +
  theme_bw()
```

## Conclusions

Standard approaches to summarizing MIC data are inadequate for datasets of this 
complexity. Treating censored observations as exact values of 256 µg/mL or discarding 
them entirely introduces bias by attenuating resistance estimates — understating how 
resistant the most resistant strains truly are. Simple summary statistics such as the 
raw median MIC ignore the multiple sources of variation present in the data, including 
day-to-day batch effects and operator-to-operator differences that, if unaccounted for, 
inflate apparent strain-level variability.

The Bayesian hierarchical framework developed here addresses each of these limitations 
simultaneously. Right-censored observations are handled naturally by evaluating the 
likelihood as P(MIC > 256) rather than treating the ceiling value as an exact 
measurement, propagating uncertainty from censored strains honestly through to the 
final posterior estimates. Operator and run variation are explicitly partitioned from 
true strain-level differences, yielding cleaner estimates of each strain's underlying 
resistance. The random slope structure of fit2 and fit3 revealed that the ETP vs CAZ 
difference is not uniform across strains — a finding that a fixed effects model would 
have missed entirely — with strains such as Pa_29605 and Pa_6214 showing dramatically 
higher ETP resistance relative to CAZ despite moderate CAZ MICs.

Across all three models the fixed and random effect estimates were stable, providing 
confidence that the results are robust to modeling choices. The progressive model 
building approach — from random intercepts only (fit1) to strain-specific antibiotic 
effects (fit2) to antibiotic-specific residual variance (fit3) — demonstrated 
meaningful improvements at each step while maintaining interpretability.

---

## Future Directions

Posterior median MIC estimates from fit3 will be prospectively validated for select 
strains by performing fresh Broth Microdilution assays from frozen stocks. Strains 
Pa_122268 and PAO1::lux have been streaked onto LB agar plates and will be retested 
to determine whether their observed MICs fall within the 95% credible intervals 
estimated by the model. This validation step will provide a direct empirical test of 
the model's predictive accuracy and will be incorporated into this document upon 
completion.

Additional future directions include fitting an ordinal model that treats MIC values 
as truly ordered categorical rather than continuous — arguably the most scientifically 
precise representation of dilution series data — and expanding the strain collection 
to include a broader range of resistance phenotypes and genotypes.
